subject: Hernia Emergencies
title: Hernia Traps: Richter's and Femoral
hook: Some hernias kill bowel while looking almost normal. These are the strangulation traps.

points.heading: Richter's: necrosis without obstruction
points.body: In a Richter's hernia only one side of the bowel wall, the antimesenteric border, is trapped. The lumen stays patent, so the bowel can become ischaemic and perforate with no obstructive symptoms.

points.heading: Femoral hernias strangulate
points.body: The femoral ring is rigid and narrow, so femoral hernias strangulate far more often than inguinal ones. They are commoner in older women, though inguinal remains the commonest hernia in women overall.

points.heading: The occult femoral hernia
points.body: Consider an occult femoral hernia in an elderly woman with small bowel obstruction and no history of abdominal surgery. A tender groin lump without obstruction may be a Richter's hernia.

points.heading: Never reduce a strangulated hernia
points.body: A strangulated hernia is tender, irreducible and has no cough impulse, with toxicity and fever. Do not try to reduce it: you risk pushing ischaemic or gangrenous bowel back into the abdomen, where it can perforate and cause peritonitis.

caption: Richter's and femoral hernias are the high-yield traps: they can necrose bowel while masking the usual obstructive signs, and a strangulated hernia must never be forced back. Suspect an occult femoral hernia in an older woman with bowel obstruction and no surgical history.
